tweets.author: WGMeets

This is real talk. As an artist most times and especially in the market that we have right now, it is really tough. We have to do whatever we gotta do to keep creating and keep getting inspired, but also somehow pay the rent.

Find what you can do to continue to be free, good https://t.co/H1pVDVuNoW

tweets.author: reneil1337
tweets.text:
While you can host these modular apps yourself we are gonna make this tech accessible via the MOCA AaaS platform. Our sustainable path forward.

Those of you who own either $moca or @ArtDeCC0s will have free access, everyone else will have to pay their subscription. Stay tuned! https://t.co/4zeHFJQA2n

tweets.author: reneil1337
tweets.text:
One goal is to make it easy for users to funnel data into their library, enabling personalized agents who remember all the info their owners curated. 

Hook your library into your claw or @NousResearch hermes agent or any other app. We enable knowledge to flow across frameworks. https://t.co/mieHdb2Phk

tweets.author: reneil1337
tweets.text: On the other end there are many ways to serve data. We got a lightweight, read-only app that plugs into your library, which of course can be remixed due to its open source nature. We encourage devs to build their own apps as the entire library stack is documented. https://t.co/3rmhs1Nn41

tweets.author: reneil1337
tweets.text: Why an app ecosystem? There are thousands of data sources. So, one of the apps allows you to paste a youtube channel url and it transcribes all videos into markdown. Shove it into your library and make new structured data available for your agents. https://t.co/YpkxqTYDhf

tweets.author: reneil1337
tweets.text:
The @MuseumofCrypto is building and I can't wait to open source the library stack. While refining the core product we create the first wave of apps which make the stack accessible.

Our Agent-as-a-Service ecosystem that resolves around knowledge and memory is taking shape.

🕳️🐇 https://t.co/kVQL5MYn7R

tweets.author: co1born
tweets.text: huge agentic innovations out of the @MuseumofCrypto lab, we’re breaking some wild new ground, worth keeping an eye on 👀💯 https://t.co/mXGxPreNF9

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text: CT could learn much from the centuries old wealth secrets of Italia https://t.co/kocHh6yilW

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
Even while painting The Sistine Chapel, Michelangelo felt out of place.

In a 1509 poem to a friend, complaining of agony, he wrote: "I am not in the right place—I am not a painter."

Here's the man himself, painted by rival Raphael, in The School of Athens.

~CdM https://t.co/g3mXacyxw0

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
Today, The Vatican Museums generate over €100M a year and pulls in 6million visitors - the Sistine Chapel is their centerpiece.

Not bad for a country of less than 900 people.

And it's not Michelangelo's only work there. 23 years later, he returned to paint The Last Judgement. https://t.co/Ce2TkaSXsQ

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
It’s easy to forget this isn’t just a museum; it’s an active, working room.

The Papal Conclave has been held here since 1492.

And since 1878, every single Pope has been elected in this exact room, directly under Michelangelo's frescoes. https://t.co/YWgrBBQtCh

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
If you visited the chapel before 1980, you will have seen a fresco darkened by centuries of candle soot and incense.

It took a 14-year restoration project to restore it back to full colour. https://t.co/qBmGp8zlKP

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
The original plan was a "simple" painting of the 12 apostles.

What it became was 343 distinct figures spread across 5,000 square feet of plaster, depicting the Book of Genesis.

This graphic helps us understand the staggering scale of the work. https://t.co/nsq36sDpGn

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
Keep in mind Michelangelo was only 33 then.

He’d already carved the 17-foot, 6-ton marble David and the breathtaking Pietà.

But when asked to paint the chapel, he balked. He considered himself a sculptor, not a painter. Eventually, he said yes. https://t.co/AYLm5jWH4S

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
That design stayed in place for almost 27 years, till 1508.

By now, there was a new Pope in town - Pope Julius II - and the Chapel was in need of renovation.

And Julius II had the perfect artist in mind to repaint the ceiling: Michelangelo https://t.co/DAjmOrMQbS

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
Before the rebuild, it's said the ceiling had no notable painting on it.

The first artist to paint the Sistine Chapel ceiling was Piermatteo d'Amelia around 1481, with blue sky, golden borders, and stars.

This drawing gives us an idea of what it may have looked like. https://t.co/zB330hNp41

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
The rebuilt chapel needed art for decoration. So, Sixtus called in the dream team.

Botticelli. Perugino. Ghirlandaio. Rosselli. Pinturicchio.

Five of the greatest painters alive — all working on the walls of the Sistine Chapel, painting stories of Christ and Moses. https://t.co/X3zVNTwSWM

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
Before it was the Sistine, a crumbling papal chapel called the Cappella Magna stood on this site. By the 1470s, it was beyond repair.

Pope Sixtus IV rebuilt it and it became known after the man himself as the Sistine Chapel. https://t.co/O8gZsQu8lq

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text:
Before 1508, The Sistine Chapel looked boring.

Then Michelangelo was hired to paint the ceiling.

This is the story of how one artist changed a country, religion, and art forever.🧵 👇 https://t.co/UAOygcMBZK

tweets.author: CozomoMedici
tweets.text: Start your morning reading this☕️ https://t.co/mQayD1TsNA
